'''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}|02={{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命|lt=Kibitsuhiko}}
［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu}}|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}|03={{ill|Mimaki-hime|ja|御間城姫|lt={{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013年}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍}} * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} * {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} [[Category:Link complete]] The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}|02={{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命|lt=Kibitsuhiko}}
［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu}}|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}|03={{ill|Mimaki-hime|ja|御間城姫|lt={{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013年}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍}} * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} * {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} [[Category:Link complete]] [[ja:武渟川別]]

'''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}|02={{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命|lt=Kibitsuhiko}}
［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu}}|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}|03={{ill|Mimaki-hime|ja|御間城姫|lt={{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013年}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍}} * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} * {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} [[Category:Link complete]] '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}|02={{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命|lt=Kibitsuhiko}}
［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu}}|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}|03={{ill|Mimaki-hime|ja|御間城姫|lt={{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013年}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍}} * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} * {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} [[Category:Link complete]] '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}|02={{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命|lt=Kibitsuhiko}}
［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu}}|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}|03={{ill|Mimaki-hime|ja|御間城姫|lt={{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013年}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍}} * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} * {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} [[Category:Link complete]] '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}|02={{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命|lt=Kibitsuhiko}}
［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu}}|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}|03={{ill|Mimaki-hime|ja|御間城姫|lt={{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013年}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍}} * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} * {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} [[Category:Link complete]] He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===

The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===

The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===

The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===

In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. revision.text:
'''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}|02={{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命|lt=Kibitsuhiko}}
［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu}}|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}|03={{ill|Mimaki-hime|ja|御間城姫|lt={{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013年}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}} * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} * {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} [[Category:Link complete]] Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}|02={{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命|lt=Kibitsuhiko}}
［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu}}|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}|03={{ill|Mimaki-hime|ja|御間城姫|lt={{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013年}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}} * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} * {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} [[Category:Link complete]] '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}|02={{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命|lt=Kibitsuhiko}}
［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu}}|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}|03={{ill|Mimaki-hime|ja|御間城姫|lt={{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013年}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}} * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} * {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} [[Category:Link complete]] In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013年}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}} * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} * {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} [[Category:Link complete]] revision.text:
'''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}|02={{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命|lt=Kibitsuhiko}}
［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu}}|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}|03={{ill|Mimaki-hime|ja|御間城姫|lt={{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013年}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}} * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} * {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} [[Category:Link complete]] The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} * {{Cite '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}|02={{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命|lt=Kibitsuhiko}}

（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}|02={{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命|lt=Kibitsuhiko}}
［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu}}|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}|03={{ill|Mimaki-hime|ja|御間城姫|lt={{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} * {{Cite '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}|02={{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命|lt=Kibitsuhiko}}
［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu}}|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}|03={{ill|Mimaki-hime|ja|御間城姫|lt={{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013年}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}} * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} * {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} [[Category:completion tier '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}.
