According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===

［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu}}|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}|03={{ill|Mimaki-hime|ja|御間城姫|lt={{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}|02={{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命|lt=Kibitsuhiko}}

（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite revision.text:
'''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}|02={{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命|lt=Kibitsuhiko}}
［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu}}|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}|03={{ill|Mimaki-hime|ja|御間城姫|lt={{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}|02={{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命|lt=Kibitsuhiko}}
［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu}}|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}|03={{ill|Mimaki-hime|ja|御間城姫|lt={{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013rd year |p=38-59}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} * {{Cite '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}|02={{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命|lt=Kibitsuhiko}}
［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu}}|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}|03={{ill|Mimaki-hime|ja|御間城姫|lt={{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013rd year |p=38-59}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013rd year }}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Japanese prince|ja|皇族}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}, a prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}, and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the {{ill|Tōkaidō region|ja|東海道}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|ja|孝元天皇}}|02={{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命|lt=Kibitsuhiko}}
［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu}}|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}|03={{ill|Mimaki-hime|ja|御間城姫|lt={{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and {{ill|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|吉備津彦命}} were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo Furune|ja|出雲振根}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko no Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day {{ill|Aizu|ja|会津}} in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} Additionally, the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏}}, rulers of the northeastern part of {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013rd year |p=38-59}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013rd year }}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the {{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 武渟川別命 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = [[古墳時代]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 別名 = 武渟川別、武渟河別、建沼河別命、建沼河別、建沼河命、武沼河別命 | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = 大夫 | 主君 = [[崇神天皇]]、[[垂仁天皇]] | 氏族 = [[阿倍氏|阿倍臣]]等祖 | 父母 = 父：[[大彦命]]『日本書紀』 | 兄弟 = [[御間城姫命]] | 妻 = | 子 = [[豊韓別命]]『本朝皇胤紹運録』、大屋田子命 | 特記事項 = }} [[File:武渟川別 Takenukawawake 日本百将伝.jpg|thumb|220px|[[歌川国芳]]『日本百将伝』より武渟川別の想像図]] '''武渟川別'''（たけぬなかわわけ、[[生没年不詳]]）は、[[記紀]]等に伝わる[[古墳時代]]の[[皇族]]。 『[[日本書紀]]』では「武渟川別」「武渟河別」、『[[古事記]]』では「'''建沼河別命'''（たけぬなかわわけのみこと）」と表記される。 第8代[[孝元天皇]][[皇子]]の[[大彦命]]の子で、[[阿倍氏|阿倍臣（阿倍氏）]]の祖。[[四道将軍]]の1人として[[東海道|東海]]に派遣されたほか、[[垂仁天皇]]朝では五大夫の1人に数えられる。 __TOC__{{-}} == 記録 ==

［東海］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[丹波道主命]]
（丹波）|02={{Supra|11}} [[垂仁天皇]]}} {{familytree/end}}
『[[日本書紀]]』崇神天皇10年9月9日条では武渟川別を[[東海道|東海]]に派遣するとあり、同書では[[北陸道|北陸]]に派遣された[[大彦命]]、[[山陽道|西道]]に派遣された[[吉備津彦命]]、[[丹波国|丹波]]に派遣された[[丹波道主命]]とともに「[[四道将軍]]」と総称されている{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}。その後、将軍らは崇神天皇10年10月22日に出発し、崇神天皇11年4月28日に平定を報告した。 また同書崇神天皇60年7月14日条によると、天皇の命により武渟川別は吉備津彦と共に[[出雲振根]]を誅殺している{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}。垂仁天皇25年2月8日条では、[[彦国葺命|彦国葺]]（[[和珥氏|和珥臣]]祖）・[[国摩大鹿島命|大鹿島]]（[[中臣氏|中臣連]]祖）・[[物部十千根|十千根]]（[[物部氏|物部連]]祖）・[[大伴武日|武日]]（[[大伴氏|大伴連]]祖）らとともに「大夫（まえつきみ）」の1人に数えられており、天皇から神祇祭祀のことを命じられている{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}。 一方『[[古事記]]』では、四道将軍としての4人の派遣ではないが、やはり崇神天皇の時に大毘古命（大彦命）は高志道に、建沼河別命は東方十二道に派遣されたとする。そして大毘古命と建沼河別命が出会った地が「相津」（現・[[福島県]][[会津]]）と名付けられた、と地名起源説話を伝える。 == 後裔氏族 == 武渟川別について、『日本書紀』では「阿倍臣遠祖」、『古事記』では「阿倍臣等の祖」として、いずれも[[阿倍氏]]の祖であるとしている。 また『[[新撰姓氏録]]』には、次の氏族が後裔として記載されている。 * 左京皇別 竹田臣 - 阿倍朝臣同祖。大彦命男の武渟川別命の後{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}。 そのほか『[[先代旧事本紀]]』「[[国造本紀]]」では、[[那須国造]]（[[栃木県]]北東部を支配）について「建沼河命の孫の[[大臣命]]」に始まると記されているが、この「建沼河命」は武渟川別に比定される「那須国」『国史大辞典』 [[吉川弘文館]]。。 == 考証 == 武渟川別が派遣された「東方十二道」は会津に至るまでの12ヶ国を意味するが、これは後世の国制を反映したものと見られる{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}}。この武渟川別を含む四道将軍の史実性の評価には諸説がある。詳しくは「[[四道将軍]]」を参照。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013年}}}} == 関連項目 == * [[飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] * [[四道将軍]] * [[伊佐須美神社]] * [[武蔵御嶽神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:たけぬなかわわけ}} [[Category:古墳時代以前の男性皇族]] [[Category:日本古代の武人・武官]] [[Category:古墳時代の人物]] [[Category:古代日本の人物]] [[Category:阿倍氏]] [[Category:生没年不詳]]

revision.text:
{{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 武渟川別命 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = [[古墳時代]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 別名 = 武渟川別、武渟河別、建沼河別命、建沼河別、建沼河命、武沼河別命 | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = 大夫 | 主君 = [[崇神天皇]]、[[垂仁天皇]] | 氏族 = [[阿倍氏|阿倍臣]]等祖 | 父母 = 父：[[大彦命]]『日本書紀』 | 兄弟 = [[御間城姫命]] | 妻 = | 子 = [[豊韓別命]]『本朝皇胤紹運録』、大屋田子命 | 特記事項 = }} [[File:武渟川別 Takenukawawake 日本百将伝.jpg|thumb|220px|[[歌川国芳]]『日本百将伝』より武渟川別の想像図]] '''武渟川別'''（たけぬなかわわけ、[[生没年不詳]]）は、[[記紀]]等に伝わる[[古墳時代]]の[[皇族]]。 『[[日本書紀]]』では「武渟川別」「武渟河別」、『[[古事記]]』では「'''建沼河別命'''（たけぬなかわわけのみこと）」と表記される。 第8代[[孝元天皇]][[皇子]]の[[大彦命]]の子で、[[阿倍氏|阿倍臣（阿倍氏）]]の祖。[[四道将軍]]の1人として[[東海道|東海]]に派遣されたほか、[[垂仁天皇]]朝では五大夫の1人に数えられる。 __TOC__{{-}} == 記録 ==
'''『[[日本書紀]]』に基づく関係系図''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[孝霊天皇]]}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} [[孝元天皇]]|02=[[吉備津彦命]]
［西道］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} [[開化天皇]]|02=[[大彦命]]
［北陸］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[彦坐王|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[崇神天皇]]|03=[[御間城姫|{{color|#FC4E6B|御間城姫}}]]|04='''武渟川別'''
［東海］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[丹波道主命]]
（丹波）|02={{Supra|11}} [[垂仁天皇]]}} {{familytree/end}}
『[[日本書紀]]』崇神天皇10年9月9日条では武渟川別を[[東海道|東海]]に派遣するとあり、同書では[[北陸道|北陸]]に派遣された[[大彦命]]、[[山陽道|西道]]に派遣された[[吉備津彦命]]、[[丹波国|丹波]]に派遣された[[丹波道主命]]とともに「[[四道将軍]]」と総称されている{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}。その後、将軍らは崇神天皇10年10月22日に出発し、崇神天皇11年4月28日に平定を報告した。 また同書崇神天皇60年7月14日条によると、天皇の命により武渟川別は吉備津彦と共に[[出雲振根]]を誅殺している{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}。垂仁天皇25年2月8日条では、[[彦国葺命|彦国葺]]（[[和珥氏|和珥臣]]祖）・[[国摩大鹿島命|大鹿島]]（[[中臣氏|中臣連]]祖）・[[物部十千根|十千根]]（[[物部氏|物部連]]祖）・[[大伴武日|武日]]（[[大伴氏|大伴連]]祖）らとともに「大夫（まえつきみ）」の1人に数えられており、天皇から神祇祭祀のことを命じられている{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}。 一方『[[古事記]]』では、四道将軍としての4人の派遣ではないが、やはり崇神天皇の時に大毘古命（大彦命）は高志道に、建沼河別命は東方十二道に派遣されたとする。そして大毘古命と建沼河別命が出会った地が「相津」（現・[[福島県]][[会津]]）と名付けられた、と地名起源説話を伝える。 == 後裔氏族 == 武渟川別について、『日本書紀』では「阿倍臣遠祖」、『古事記』では「阿倍臣等の祖」として、いずれも[[阿倍氏]]の祖であるとしている。 また『[[新撰姓氏録]]』には、次の氏族が後裔として記載されている。 * 左京皇別 竹田臣 - 阿倍朝臣同祖。大彦命男の武渟川別命の後{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}。 そのほか『[[先代旧事本紀]]』「[[国造本紀]]」では、[[那須国造]]（[[栃木県]]北東部を支配）について「建沼河命の孫の[[大臣命]]」に始まると記されているが、この「建沼河命」は武渟川別に比定される「那須国」『国史大辞典』 [[吉川弘文館]]。。 == 考証 == 武渟川別が派遣された「東方十二道」は会津に至るまでの12ヶ国を意味するが、これは後世の国制を反映したものと見られる{{Sfn|宝賀|2013年|p=38-59}}。この武渟川別を含む四道将軍の史実性の評価には諸説がある。詳しくは「[[四道将軍]]」を参照。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013年}}}} == 関連項目 == * [[飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] * [[四道将軍]] * [[伊佐須美神社]] * [[武蔵御嶽神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:たけぬなかわわけ}} [[Category:古墳時代以前の男性皇族]] [[Category:日本古代の武人・武官]] [[Category:古墳時代の人物]] [[Category:古代日本の人物]] [[Category:阿倍氏]] [[Category:生没年不詳]]

revision.text:
[[ja:武渟川別]][[zh:武渟川别]] {{Infobox person|image=File:武渟川別 Takenukawawake 日本百将伝.jpg|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|qid=Q2658252}}|children={{ill|Abe kogoto|qid=Q97613711}}; {{ill|Q109290210|qid=Q109290210}}|father={{ill|Prince Ōhiko|qid=Q11435420}}|relatives={{ill|Empress Mimakihime|qid=Q11071228}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11546050]] Wikidata name: Takenunakawawake [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Imperial Prince|lt=Imperial Prince|lt=Japanese prince|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese prince|qq=}} from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, a prince of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]], and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the [[:en:Tōkaidō (region)|Tōkaidō region]]. During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]]}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[:en:Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko]]
［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoimasu|qq=}}|02={{Supra|10}} [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[:en:Mimaki-hime|{{color]]}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|qq=}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), [[:en:Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]] (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]] were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo no Furune|ja|出雲振根|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Furune|qq=}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko-no-Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day [[:en:Aizu|Aizu]] in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/place-name origin stories|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko-no-Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} Additionally, the ''[[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nasu clan|qq=}}, rulers of the northeastern part of [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013rd year |p=38-59}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013rd year }}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|qq=|1=List of ancient Japanese figures|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665973}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}} * [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] * {{ill|Musashi-Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Mitake Shrine|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|武渟川別|version=103240029|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] }} [[ja:武渟川別]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]][[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[zh:武渟川别]] {{wikidata link|Q11546050}}

[[ja:武渟川別]][[zh:武渟川别]] {{Infobox person|image=File:武渟川別 Takenukawawake 日本百将伝.jpg|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|WD=Q2658252}}|children={{ill|Abe kogoto|WD=Q97613711}}; {{ill|Q109290210|WD=Q109290210}}|father={{ill|Prince Ōhiko|WD=Q11435420}}|relatives={{ill|Empress Mimakihime|WD=Q11071228}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11546050]] Wikidata name: Takenunakawawake [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Imperial Prince|lt=Imperial Prince|lt=Japanese prince|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese prince|qq=}} from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, a prince of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]], and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the [[:en:Tōkaidō (region)|Tōkaidō region]]. During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===

（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), [[:en:Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]] (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]] were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo no Furune|ja|出雲振根|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Furune|qq=}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko-no-Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day [[:en:Aizu|Aizu]] in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/place-name origin stories|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko-no-Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} Additionally, the ''[[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nasu clan|qq=}}, rulers of the northeastern part of [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013rd year |p=38-59}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013rd year }}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|qq=|1=List of ancient Japanese figures|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665973}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}} * [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] * {{ill|Musashi-Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Mitake Shrine|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|武渟川別|version=103240029|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] }} [[ja:武渟川別]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]][[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[zh:武渟川别]] {{wikidata link|Q11546050}}

［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoimasu|qq=}}|02={{Supra|10}} [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[:en:Mimaki-hime|{{color]]}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|qq=}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), [[:en:Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]] (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]] were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo no Furune|ja|出雲振根|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Furune|qq=}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko-no-Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day [[:en:Aizu|Aizu]] in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/place-name origin stories|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko-no-Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} Additionally, the ''[[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nasu clan|qq=}}, rulers of the northeastern part of [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013rd year |p=38-59}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013rd year }}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|1=List of ancient Japanese figures|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665973}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}} * [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] * {{ill|Musashi-Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Mitake Shrine|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|武渟川別|version=103240029|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] }} [[ja:武渟川別]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]][[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[zh:武渟川别]] {{wikidata link|Q11546050}}

Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the [[:en:Tōkaidō (region)|Tōkaidō region]]. During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===

The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013rd year |p=38-59}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013rd year }}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|1=List of ancient Japanese figures|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665973}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}} * [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] * {{ill|Musashi-Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Mitake Shrine|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|武渟川別|version=103240029|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] }} [[ja:武渟川別]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]][[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[zh:武渟川别]] {{wikidata link|Q11546050}}

revision.text:
[[ja:武渟川別]][[zh:武渟川别]] {{Infobox person|image=File:武渟川別 Takenukawawake 日本百将伝.jpg|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|WD=Q2658252}}|children={{ill|Abe kogoto|WD=Q97613711}}; {{ill|Q109290210|WD=Q109290210}}|father={{ill|Prince Ōhiko|WD=Q11435420}}|relatives={{ill|Empress Mimakihime|WD=Q11071228}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11546050]] Wikidata name: Takenunakawawake [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Imperial Prince|lt=Imperial Prince|lt=Japanese prince|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese prince|qq=}} from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, a prince of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]], and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the [[:en:Tōkaidō (region)|Tōkaidō region]]. During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]]}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[:en:Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko]]
［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoimasu|qq=}}|02={{Supra|10}} [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[:en:Mimaki-hime|{{color]]}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|qq=}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), [[:en:Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]] (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]] were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo no Furune|ja|出雲振根|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Furune|qq=}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko-no-Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day [[:en:Aizu|Aizu]] in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/place-name origin stories|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko-no-Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} Additionally, the ''[[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nasu clan|qq=}}, rulers of the northeastern part of [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013rd year |p=38-59}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013rd year }}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|1=List of ancient Japanese figures|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665973}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}} * [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] * {{ill|Musashi-Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Mitake Shrine|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|武渟川別|version=103240029|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] }} [[ja:武渟川別]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]][[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[zh:武渟川别]] {{wikidata link|Q11546050}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoimasu|qq=}}|02={{Supra|10}} [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[:en:Mimaki-hime|{{color]]}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|qq=}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), [[:en:Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]] (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]] were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo no Furune|ja|出雲振根|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Furune|qq=}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko-no-Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day [[:en:Aizu|Aizu]] in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/place-name origin stories|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko-no-Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} Additionally, the ''[[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nasu clan|qq=}}, rulers of the northeastern part of [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013rd year |p=38-59}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013rd year }}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|1=List of ancient Japanese figures|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665973}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}} * [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] * {{ill|Musashi-Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Mitake Shrine|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|武渟川別|version=103240029|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] }} [[ja:武渟川別]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]][[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[zh:武渟川别]] {{wikidata link|Q11546050}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[ja:武渟川別]][[zh:武渟川别]] {{Infobox person|image=File:武渟川別 Takenukawawake 日本百将伝.jpg|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|WD=Q2658252}}|children={{ill|Abe kogoto|WD=Q97613711}}; {{ill|Q109290210|WD=Q109290210}}|father={{ill|Prince Ōhiko|WD=Q11435420}}|relatives={{ill|Empress Mimakihime|WD=Q11071228}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11546050]] Wikidata name: Takenunakawawake [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Imperial Prince|lt=Imperial Prince|lt=Japanese prince|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese prince|qq=}} from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, a prince of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]], and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the [[:en:Tōkaidō (region)|Tōkaidō region]]. During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===

The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013rd year |p=38-59}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013rd year }}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|1=List of ancient Japanese figures|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665973}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}} * [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] * {{ill|Musashi-Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Mitake Shrine|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|武渟川別|version=103240029|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] }} [[ja:武渟川別]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]][[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[zh:武渟川别]] {{wikidata link|Q11546050}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[ja:武渟川別]][[zh:武渟川别]] {{Infobox person|image=File:武渟川別 Takenukawawake 日本百将伝.jpg|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|WD=Q2658252}}|children={{ill|Abe kogoto|WD=Q97613711}}; {{ill|Q109290210|WD=Q109290210}}|father={{ill|Prince Ōhiko|WD=Q11435420}}|relatives={{ill|Empress Mimakihime|WD=Q11071228}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11546050]] Wikidata name: Takenunakawawake [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Takenukawawake''' (武渟川別命, ''Takenukawawake''), also referred to as ''Takenunakawawake'' or ''Takenukawawake no Mikoto,'' is a legendary {{ill|Imperial Prince|lt=Imperial Prince|lt=Japanese prince|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese prince|qq=}} from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' refers to him as "Takenukawawake" or "Takenukawabake," while the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' uses the name "Takenunakawawake no Mikoto" (建沼河別命). He is known as a son of {{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, a prince of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]], and is regarded as the ancestor of the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}}. Takenukawawake was one of the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}}, who were dispatched to pacify different regions of Japan. He was sent to the [[:en:Tōkaidō (region)|Tōkaidō region]]. During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
'''『[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]』-Based Genealogical Chart''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]]}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|8}} [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[:en:Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko]]
［Western Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02| 01={{Supra|9}} [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]|02={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}
［Northern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01={{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoimasu|qq=}}|02={{Supra|10}} [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[:en:Mimaki-hime|{{color]]}}|04='''Take Nunakawawake'''
［Eastern Route］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01={{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Michinushi-no-Mikoto|qq=}}
（Tanba Region）|02={{Supra|11}} [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]}} {{familytree/end}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' states that Takenukawawake was sent to the Tōkaidō region on September 9 of the 10th year of Emperor Sujin's reign. He is mentioned alongside {{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} (dispatched to the Hokuriku region), [[:en:Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]] (sent to the Sanyō region), and {{ill|Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|ja|丹波道主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto|qq=}} (sent to Tanba Province). These four commanders were collectively called the {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}}.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} Takenukawawake and his fellow commanders began their expeditions on October 22 and successfully reported the pacification of their assigned regions on April 28 of the following year. In the 60th year of Emperor Sujin's reign, Takenukawawake and [[:en:Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]] were ordered to execute {{ill|Izumo no Furune|ja|出雲振根|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Furune|qq=}} for treason. During the reign of Emperor Suinin, he was one of five ''maetsukimi'' tasked with overseeing religious rituals and ensuring harmony between the imperial court and the deities.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} === ''Kojiki'' Account === The ''Kojiki'' similarly mentions Takenukawawake's involvement during Emperor Sujin's reign but describes his expedition differently. According to this account, he and Ōhiko-no-Mikoto met in a place called "Aitsu" (modern-day [[:en:Aizu|Aizu]] in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]), which was named in reference to their encounter. This tale is part of a series of {{ill|place-name origin stories|ja|地名起源説話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/place-name origin stories|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. == Descendants == Takenukawawake is recognized as the ancestor of the Abe clan. The ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' specifically lists the following clans as his descendants: * '''Takeda no Atai''' (竹田臣) – Recognized as a descendant of Takenukawawake through Ōhiko-no-Mikoto.{{Sfn|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }} Additionally, the ''[[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'' states that the {{ill|Nasu clan|ja|那須氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nasu clan|qq=}}, rulers of the northeastern part of [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], trace their origins to Takenukawawake.{{Sfn|国史大辞典}} == Historical Evaluation == Takenukawawake's mission to the "Eastern Twelve Regions" may reflect later administrative structures rather than historical reality. The historicity of the Four Commanders of the Four Roads remains a topic of debate.{{Sfn|宝賀|2013rd year |p=38-59}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=武渟川別|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|武渟川別（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=[[青垣出版]]|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013rd year }}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Figures Before the Asuka Period|1=List of ancient Japanese figures|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665973}} * {{ill|Four Commanders of the Four Roads|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Commanders of the Four Roads|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}} * [[:en:Isasumi Shrine|Isasumi Shrine]] * {{ill|Musashi-Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Mitake Shrine|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|武渟川別|version=103240029|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] }} [[ja:武渟川別]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]][[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[zh:武渟川别]] {{wikidata link|Q11546050}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], he was recognized as one of the five ''maetsukimi'' (大夫), or "chief retainers." == Historical Records == === Genealogy ===
